label: OECD Effective Carbon Rate benchmarks
description: The OECD tracks carbon pricing across 79 countries by combining emissions trading system prices, carbon taxes, and fuel excise taxes into a single effective carbon rate (EUR/tCO2). These benchmarks represent the carbon price levels consistent with different decarbonisation pathways.

slow_decarbonisation.label: Slow decarbonisation pathway
slow_decarbonisation.description: Minimum pricing consistent with gradual emissions reduction. Many OECD countries are below this for non-road sectors.

net_zero_mid_century.label: Net zero by mid-century pathway
net_zero_mid_century.description: Carbon price consistent with Paris Agreement goals. Only a handful of countries price carbon this high across all sectors.

actual_averages_2023.description: Average effective carbon rates across OECD countries by source (2023 data).

carbon_taxes.note: Explicit carbon taxes where they exist

country_examples.description: Effective carbon rates for selected countries (all sectors average, EUR/tCO2, 2023).
country_examples.note: These are approximate all-sector averages. Road transport rates are typically 3-5x higher than industry or electricity due to fuel excise. Country values rounded from OECD Effective Carbon Rates 2025.

usage_in_cba: When appraising a project with carbon impacts, compare the carbon value used in your CBA against the OECD benchmarks. If your appraisal uses a carbon price below EUR 60/tCO2, note that this is below the OECD net-zero pathway benchmark. If above EUR 120/tCO2 (like the UK DESNZ or EIB shadow prices), note that this exceeds the OECD benchmark, reflecting a more ambitious decarbonisation trajectory.

source.publication: Effective Carbon Rates 2025: Pricing Greenhouse Gas Emissions through Taxes and Emissions Trading

caveats: Effective carbon rates combine ETS prices, carbon taxes, and fuel excise. Fuel excise is the largest component for most countries, but it is not set as climate policy.
All-sector averages mask large variation: road transport is priced much higher than industry or electricity in most countries.
Country examples are approximate averages. The OECD database has detailed sector-by-sector rates.
The benchmarks (EUR 60/120) are OECD staff estimates of price levels consistent with decarbonisation pathways, not binding policy targets.
